planting.note: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep alluvial or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; Oglethorpe oak grows naturally in moist, rich bottomland woods and ravine slopes of a very small native range in Georgia and South Carolina. It is extremely rare in the wild.

directions: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy or alluvial soil in partial shade to full sun; Oglethorpe oak is one of the rarest native oaks in North America, known from only a handful of scattered populations in bottomland and ravine forests of northern Georgia and adjacent South Carolina — the total wild population numbers only a few thousand trees. Taxonomically unusual within oaks, it produces very small, rounded acorns on distinctively long stalks, and the leaves are large, dark green, and deeply lobulate with rounded tips. The species was not scientifically described until 1940, found in a ravine near Oglethorpe County, Georgia. Despite its extreme rarity in the wild, it grows vigorously in cultivation and has proven adaptable to a wider range of conditions than its limited natural distribution suggests. A conservation-worthy ornamental that horticultural planting can help save from extinction. Fast-growing and handsome, with good fall color and excellent form.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees or cuttings in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; shortleaf fig grows naturally in tropical hardwood hammocks and coastal strand of south Florida and the Keys, often germinating in the crevices of strangler fig fashion — growing down aerial roots to eventually envelope host trees.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; shortleaf fig is the native banyan tree of south Florida and the Florida Keys — a hemiepiphytic strangler fig in the Moraceae family that begins life as an epiphyte in the crevices of trees or walls before sending down aerial roots that fuse around the host, eventually forming a trunk that can outlive the original host. Related to and similar in growth habit to the more commonly planted Florida strangler fig (Ficus aurea), shortleaf fig is distinguished by its somewhat smaller, more oval-rounded leaves with a distinctive drip tip. The small, rounded figs produced on short stalks are consumed by white-crowned pigeons, mockingbirds, and other subtropical birds. A critically important food source for south Florida's native bird community. More cold-sensitive than strangler fig, and restricted to zone 10–12. Used in native-plant gardening in Dade and Monroe Counties for habitat restoration.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, dry to average, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; Santa Fe phlox grows naturally on dry, rocky limestone and igneous slopes and desert grasslands of the Chihuahuan Desert and southern Rocky Mountain foothills of New Mexico and west Texas.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry to average, rocky or sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Santa Fe phlox is a low-growing native phlox of dry, rocky desert grasslands and limestone slopes of New Mexico, west Texas, and northern Chihuahua, producing clusters of fragrant, rose-pink, lavender, or white flowers in spring. The low, somewhat spreading, semi-evergreen habit makes it well-suited to dry, rocky gardens and the xeriscape. More heat and drought tolerant than most phlox — adapted to the extreme summer conditions of the Chihuahuan Desert where temperatures regularly exceed 100°F — making it valuable for dry gardens in zones 7–9 where other phlox struggle in summer heat. Several named cultivars with improved flower size and color, including selections in deep magenta-pink, are available from xeric native plant nurseries. An excellent pollinator plant for native bees in spring and a good nectar source for tiger swallowtail butterflies.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring along stream banks, seep margins, or wet meadow edges in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, mineral or loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; purple monkeyflower requires consistently moist to wet soil and cool summer temperatures, growing naturally along cold mountain streams and wet subalpine meadows.

directions: Plant or direct sow in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, mineral-rich soil along stream banks, seeps, or wet meadow margins in partial shade to full sun; purple monkeyflower (formerly Mimulus lewisii) is a native perennial of cold mountain streams and subalpine wet meadows from the Rockies through the Cascades, producing large, showy, rose-pink to deep magenta flowers with yellow-bearded lower lips and dark spotting — among the most beautiful of the wild monkeyflowers. Named after Meriwether Lewis of the Lewis and Clark expedition, who collected it during the crossing of the Rockies. The large, tubular flowers are primarily pollinated by bumblebees, but at sites where the species co-occurs with its red-flowered relative E. cardinalis, the two species have been the subject of landmark research demonstrating how pollinator preference drives flower color evolution. An outstanding native for boggy streamside or pond-edge gardens in cool climate zones.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in full sun to partial shade; northern bedstraw grows naturally in open prairies, meadow edges, rocky stream banks, and open woodlands across northern North America and Eurasia. Seeds benefit from cold stratification.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, rocky or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; northern bedstraw is an attractive native perennial of prairies, open rocky meadows, and woodland edges across northern North America and Eurasia, producing whorled leaves in groups of four and dense clusters of tiny, bright white, fragrant flowers in early to midsummer — among the most floriferous of the native Galium species. Unlike the scrambling, sticky-stemmed cleavers (G. aparine), northern bedstraw is upright and well-mannered, forming attractive clumps that spread slowly by rhizomes. The dried plant was historically used to stuff mattresses (giving the genus its common name 'bedstraw'), and the seeds can be roasted as a coffee substitute — the genus Galium is closely related to coffee (Coffea). The small white flowers are heavily visited by small native bees, flower flies, and beneficial wasps. A good, underused native for dry to average, rocky meadow gardens.
